title: The Amazing Benefits of Honey for Insulin Sensitivity

chunks.title: Discussion on Honey
chunks.summary: The video starts with a conversation about honey, specifically marola honey, and its health benefits. It emphasizes that honey can improve insulin sensitivity and testosterone levels, countering recent criticisms regarding its health implications.
chunks.transcript: and I've even seen people attacking honey yeah and you and I we we geek out on honey because I I brought you this honey from my house in Costa Rica it called marola honey yeah dude oh I so bum that you didn't bring it sting bees I'm bringing it next time stingless bees those are my kind of bees they they're amazing so I have a hive in my house marola honey but there's been people attacking honey recently but if you look at the medical literature you can't attack honey yeah I mean honey improves insulin sensitivity sucrose not so much which is you know this pure molecule that's been extracted from the honey honey has over 300 bioactive compounds yeah but honey improves metabolic function in in humans and honey improves testosterone and there's review Papers written about honey for metabolic illness it's it's wild and we get so myopic in medicine kind of like with the LDL we just focus on blood glucose right blood glucose is a symptom of underlying metabolic dysfunction it's not the metabolic dysfunction right eating honey does not cause insulin resistance honey doesn't cause diabetes right if have diabetes honey is going to spike your blood sugar but it didn't cause it right so if you have diabetes yeah don't eat as much honey as you or I but small amounts of Honey probably beneficial and honey didn't cause the problem we have to point we have to point the weapons at the actual cause right if you point the weapon at the wrong cause you're just putting your resources where they shouldn't be and as we talked about APO B wrong place to put your weapon right

chunks.title: Metabolic Dysfunction
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights the misconception that blood glucose levels are the sole indicator of metabolic health. Instead, it stresses that underlying metabolic dysfunction should be the focus, and honey itself does not cause diabetes or insulin resistance.

chunks.title: Fructose Metabolism in Humans vs. Animals
chunks.summary: The segment provides insights into fructose metabolism, contrasting the human body's response to fructose with that of animals. It argues that humans do not convert fructose to fat as animals do, and the results from animal studies on fructose cannot be directly applied to humans.
chunks.transcript: you give fructose to a rat it's making much more of that into body fat than than we do as a human we do 1% you give a human 1% of fructose goes to Fat via denova lipogenesis most of it goes to glucose it goes to um you know glycogen it goes to lactate like glucose I mean fructose doesn't turn into fat in humans anywhere near like it does in animals so you can't look at animal studies for

chunks.title: The Issues with Isolated Fructose Feeding
chunks.summary: The video explains the potential negative effects of isolated fructose feeding, particularly its relation to gut health. It discusses how free fructose can lead to overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria, causing endotoxin release, which may not occur when consuming whole fruits.
chunks.transcript: fructose metabolism because it's completely different right it's completely different and if you look at human studies with fructose they are as you suggest invariably giving pure fructose there's not a single fruit or honey or anything on the planet where you're getting pure fructose without glucose right and in the gut it's all sorters it's all Transporters that need glucose and fructose to move across the gut lining yeah and so if you get free fructose in the gut it gets stuck in the gut well what happens if fructose gets stuck in the gut it causes overgrowth of pathogenic bacteria and then you get lipop you get you get lipopolysaccharide you get endotoxin so they've done these studies in animals where they'll give free fructose and they'll give something that blocks tlr four or five to like receptor four or five which is what endotoxin triggers eventually and the negative effects of fructose are essentially abolished wow so the negative effects of fructose at the level of the gut and humans is driven by endotoxin and we don't get more antitoxin when we eat fruit in fact I think you could easily make a case that when you eat a blueberry or you're eating a Pon or you're eating orange juice and a toxin which is lipopolysaccharide goes down but the harmful effects of fructose in humans are isolated fructose feeding leading to LPS so it's just it's again it really

chunks.title: Addressing Misconceptions in Nutrition Research
chunks.summary: The final segment reflects on the importance of considering all research comprehensively and acknowledges the diverse opinions within the health community. It calls for collaboration among experts to prevent narrow conclusions based on selective data interpretation.
